body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

Reassessment of a previously-`bug` cell against opencode v1.14.50 and the current daemon (opencode/watcher.go `scanSessions`, opencode/metrics.go). **The prior `daemon_capability: bug` was mislabeled and is now stale — it flips to `full`.**

## What this scenario actually tests

The primitive is *multi-adapter coexistence*: a DIFFERENT-adapter set (claudecode + codex + pi + aider) and one opencode session live in the SAME cwd at once, and the daemon must label each adapter independently with fully independent state/metrics and no cross-contamination. The user-observable signal is: each session appears with its correct adapter label; states transition independently; no merging/flapping of metrics, parent linkage, or subagent counts.

## Why the daemon is `full` here

The transports are disjoint at the source-variant level, so no shared reader/parser/table can leak state:
1. **Process matching** — opencode declares `ExactName{Name: opencode}` (agent.go); each adapter's matcher runs independently and tags each PID with its originating adapter.
2. **Session store** — opencode is the only `ProcessOwnedStore` adapter; its watcher keys every session by the SQLite `session.id` column (`ses_…`), NOT by cwd. `scanSessions` does `SELECT id, directory, … FROM session` and stores `w.cursors[s.id]` per id (watcher.go ~L257–L326); `ComputeMetrics` queries `WHERE p.session_id = ?` (metrics.go ~L67–L73). opencode.db only ever holds opencode rows, disjoint from the partners' FilesUnderRoot (claudecode/codex/pi JSONL) and FilesUnderCWD (aider markdown) transports.
3. **Per-(adapter, session_id) state** — metrics and parent linkage key off the (adapter, session_id) tuple via the maps.go projections; there is no global per-cwd aggregation.

The existing recording's `events.jsonl` is direct evidence the coexistence spec is met: five DISTINCT sessions appear, each with its correct adapter label — opencode `ses_19ea3aac2ffe5C0USJRj6zD2gL` (adapter opencode), claude-code `1dddc799-…`, codex `rollout-2026-05-26T00-58-19-…`, pi `2026-05-25T22-58-18-…`, aider `proc-28672` — each with its own independent ready→working transition (seq 735 for opencode, 723/736 for claude-code, 775/776 for codex, 786/787 for pi, 738/780 for aider). The daemon never merges or flaps one adapter's row into another's. This is the load-bearing assertion and it passes cleanly.

This matches the sibling `multiple-sessions-same-cwd` reassessment, which found the daemon keys opencode sessions by `session.id` (not cwd) and is therefore `daemon_capability: full` — two sessions are independently observable. The same keying makes two DIFFERENT adapters in one cwd trivially independent.

## Why the old `bug` was stale

The prior verdict downgraded this cell to `bug` over a single failing assertion: `agentB_turn_done` (the opencode session's working→ready settle). The recording shows WHY that one assertion truncated, and it is NOT a multi-adapter bug:
- opencode `ses_19ea3aac…` last `transcript_activity` at seq 804 (00:59:32.582);
- `process_exited` pid=28670 at seq 805 (00:59:32.722) — only ~140 ms later;
- the terminal `step-finish=stop` part is not read by the ProcessOwnedStore poller until seq 807 (00:59:38.094) — 5.5 s after the process already exited.

So there is no working→ready `state_transition` for the opencode row: the single-shot `opencode run` process exited before the poller's tick read the terminal part. This is opencode's GENERIC headless single-shot fast-exit truncation (the same mechanism captured for pi/turn-aborted-by-error and opencode's own headless settle behaviour), and the standalone `basic-turn` opencode fixture proves the daemon DOES emit working→ready when the poller's tick wins the race (opencode runs alone there → `turn1_done: ready`, no known_failing). It is therefore a settle-timing/driver concern, not a spec-required observable of the multi-adapter primitive — which the SKILL's authoring rule says belongs in `caveats`, keeping `daemon_capability: full`. Parking this generic settle-race in `bug` on the multi-agent cell is exactly the defensive-`bug` mislabel #476 set out to kill.

## Driver

The opencode interactive driver supports send/wait_turn/sleep and honors `$IRRLICHT_ONBOARD_CWD`, so it shares the run-cell-multi.sh workspace with the partner adapters. The recipe (one short text turn) is exactly the shape this cell needs → `driver_capability: ready`. The agentB settle truncation is a property of single-shot `opencode run` exiting at turn-end; a persistent-session driver mode that outlives the settle would also clear the trailing arc, but it is not required for the coexistence assertions.

## Route

record — re-record (or re-promote) the cross-adapter fixture as a PASSING fixture for the load-bearing coexistence assertions and DROP the `known_failing` flag from expected.jsonl. The agentB working→ready settle is a caveat (opencode's headless fast-exit), not a daemon mishandling of this scenario.
caveats: opencode's `agentB_turn_done` working->ready settle can truncate: single-shot `opencode run` exits ~140 ms after the final transcript_activity (events.jsonl seq 805), before the ProcessOwnedStore poller reads the terminal step-finish=stop part (seq 807, +5.5 s). This is opencode's generic headless fast-exit settle race (cf. basic-turn captures it standalone when the poller's tick wins), NOT a multi-adapter merge/cross-contamination bug — the load-bearing coexistence assertions all pass.

opencode's default model (a slow local LM Studio model, ~30-45 s/turn) means its working span is long and it may enter working AFTER a faster partner; the spec is adapter-agnostic (agentA = first to working, agentB = the other), so either ordering validates.
Adapter labels are stable wire strings (opencode, claude-code, codex, pi, aider); the assertion is on the wire-level adapter name, not the macOS display string.

A persistent-session opencode driver mode (the -i TUI, not currently driver-supported) that outlives the turn would also clear the agentB settle arc — but it is not required for the coexistence primitive this cell tests.

sources.note: scanSessions SELECTs `id, directory, time_updated, parent_id FROM session` and keys cursors by `w.cursors[s.id]` (the SQLite session.id, ses_…), NOT by cwd — so two sessions (or a session next to a different adapter) in one cwd are independently observable. No state shared with any file-based adapter.
ComputeMetrics / querySessionMetrics query `WHERE p.session_id = ?` — metrics are per session.id, no per-cwd aggregation that could merge a partner adapter's tokens/model.

Five distinct adapter-labeled sessions each take an independent ready->working transition (opencode seq 735, claude-code 736, codex 775/776, pi 786/787, aider 780) with no merge/flap — the load-bearing coexistence assertion passes; daemon_capability=full.

Proof the single failing assertion is a headless fast-exit race, not a merge bug: opencode session last activity seq 804 (00:59:32.582) -> process_exited seq 805 (00:59:32.722, +140ms) -> terminal step-finish read seq 807 (00:59:38.094, +5.5s), so no working->ready state_transition was emitted for ses_19ea3aac. Belongs in caveats, not bug.

Standalone opencode turn1_done asserts expected_state=ready with NO known_failing — proves the daemon DOES emit working->ready when opencode runs alone (poller tick wins the race). Confirms the multi-agent settle truncation is a timing/driver concern, not daemon incapacity.
Sibling reassessment found the daemon keys opencode sessions by session.id (not cwd) -> daemon_capability: full. The same keying makes two DIFFERENT adapters in one cwd independent here.
Parsers / PIDDiscoverers / ProcessNames / MetricsProviders are keyed by adapter Identity.Name — opencode's MetricsReader is registered only for opencode sessions; disjoint dispatch per adapter.
